Title: On the significance of an alternate pathway of melatonin synthesis via 5-methoxytryptamine: comparisons across species

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal two-step branch from serotonin/5-methoxytryptamine to melatonin
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step in the alternate pathway; penultimate step in the classic pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to serotonin or 5-methoxytryptamine; in the alternate pathway acetylates 5-methoxytryptamine to melatonin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Proposed last enzyme and possible rate-limiting enzyme in plants when the alternate pathway prevails
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes melatonin accumulation; evidence supports that plant SNAT preferentially uses 5-methoxytryptamine, favoring the alternate route to melatonin
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis SNAT showed substantially higher affinity and catalytic efficiency for 5-methoxytryptamine than for serotonin. 2. These kinetics support a reversed terminal sequence in plants, where 5-methoxytryptamine is acetylated to melatonin. 3. The paper argues that SNAT, not ASMT, may be the last enzyme of melatonin synthesis in some plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme kinetic assay; substrate specificity comparison
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target to enhance melatonin content in plants, but isoform-specific differences and species dependence should be considered
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review/hypothesis paper; conclusions are drawn from published enzyme kinetics and caution is noted because multiple SNAT isoforms may differ among plant species.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal O-methylation branch from serotonin or N-acetylserotonin
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step in the classic pathway; upstream O-methylation step in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates N-acetylserotonin to melatonin and can also methylate serotonin to 5-methoxytryptamine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in the classic pathway; forms immediate precursor in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established in plants; proposed not to be the final determinant when the alternate pathway predominates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to melatonin biosynthesis but may preferentially methylate serotonin to 5-methoxytryptamine in Arabidopsis, supporting the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Recombinant Arabidopsis ASMT catalyzed both N-acetylserotonin to melatonin and serotonin to 5-methoxytryptamine. 2. The reported Vmax with serotonin was higher than with N-acetylserotonin. 3. These data support the hypothesis that ASMT can function before SNAT in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme activity assay; substrate comparison
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering melatonin biosynthesis, but isoform diversity and uncertain predominance across species require caution
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Plant ASMTs occur as multiple isoforms; the paper emphasizes limited interspecies comparison and cautions against overgeneralization.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream precursor-forming step in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies 5-methoxytryptamine precursor for melatonin synthesis via the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis COMT was reported to convert serotonin to 5-methoxytryptamine. 2. This provides an alternative O-methylation entry into melatonin biosynthesis. 3. Together with SNAT substrate preference, COMT supports the proposed predominance of the 5-methoxytryptamine route in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzymatic evidence cited in the review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential stacking target with SNAT/ASMT to boost melatonin biosynthesis via precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the paper explicitly highlights Arabidopsis COMT as a contributor to 5-methoxytryptamine formation.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates 5-methoxytryptamine or serotonin, with much higher catalytic efficiency toward 5-methoxytryptamine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports melatonin biosynthesis through preferential acetylation of 5-methoxytryptamine, indicating the alternate pathway likely prevails in cyanobacteria
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Recombinant cyanobacterial cbSNAT showed 14.2-fold greater catalytic efficiency with 5-methoxytryptamine than with serotonin. 2. This strongly supports the existence of the alternate melatonin pathway in cyanobacteria. 3. The paper links cyanobacterial SNAT to plant melatonin pathway evolution.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful evolutionary and synthetic biology template for engineering melatonin production
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper notes amino-acid homology between cyanobacterial cbSNAT and plant SNATs, suggesting evolutionary inheritance.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates 5-methoxytryptamine or serotonin, with higher catalytic efficiency toward 5-methoxytryptamine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports a likely predominant alternate melatonin pathway in yeast by preferential use of 5-methoxytryptamine
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cloned yeast scAANAT displayed about sevenfold greater catalytic efficiency with 5-methoxytryptamine than with serotonin. 2. These enzyme kinetics align with earlier depletion/restoration evidence showing strong melatonin increases after 5-methoxytryptamine addition. 3. The review concludes that scAANAT is likely the ultimate enzyme for melatonin production in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; structural analysis; recombinant enzyme kinetics; precursor feeding/depletion-restoration evidence cited
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Starvation/salt medium depletion and precursor addition in cited yeast studies
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for microbial melatonin bioproduction and industrial biosynthesis platforms
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review discusses engineered yeast strains for high melatonin production, but those transgenes are from mixed origins and are not the main summarized core gene in this paper.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Classic terminal acetylation branch and possible alternate terminal acetylation branch
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: penultimate step in the classic pathway; terminal step in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AANAT family; vertebrate forms possess regulatory N- and C-terminal flanking regions
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acetylates serotonin to N-acetylserotonin in the classic pathway and can acetylate 5-methoxytryptamine to melatonin in the alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in alternate pathway; forms immediate precursor in classic pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Widely accepted as rate-limiting in vertebrate pineal gland, though not under all conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Usually supports melatonin synthesis through the classic route in vertebrate pineal gland, but kinetic data in some animals suggest capacity to use 5-methoxytryptamine and contribute to an alternate route
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AANAT is the established key enzyme of classic vertebrate melatonin synthesis and often rate-limiting in pineal gland. 2. Comparative kinetic data indicate that some animal AANATs, including human, sheep, cockroach, and Drosophila enzymes, can preferentially use 5-methoxytryptamine over serotonin under tested conditions. 3. The review proposes that alternate-route contribution may occur in some animals, especially extrapineal sites and invertebrates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative enzyme kinetics from multiple cited species; historical cloning and biochemical studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Circadian in pineal gland and retina in vertebrates
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/dark cycle; photic runoff; pH conditions in comparative enzyme assays
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for metabolic engineering of melatonin in crops or microbes, but tissue context and species-specific enzyme kinetics matter
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a broad review-level summary rather than a single-species experimental claim; evidence for alternate-route predominance in animals remains tentative.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Classic terminal methylation branch and alternate upstream O-methylation branch
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step in classic pathway; upstream precursor-forming step in alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates N-acetylserotonin to melatonin and can also methylate serotonin to 5-methoxytryptamine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in classic pathway; forms immediate precursor in alternate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: May become rate-limiting at highest nocturnal synthetic rates; not generally considered the main control point
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Usually assigned to final melatonin synthesis in the classic pathway, but divergent activity data with serotonin raise the possibility of participation in an alternate route in some tissues or species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ASMT is the canonical final enzyme of classic melatonin synthesis. 2. Comparative reports of enzyme activity with serotonin versus N-acetylserotonin are divergent, partly due to methodological limitations and possible masking by MAO-mediated 5-methoxytryptamine degradation. 3. The review argues that ASMT substrate preference in animals remains insufficiently resolved and may permit alternate-pathway contribution.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative enzymology from cited literature; tissue homogenate activity comparisons
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/dark cycle; MAO inhibitor context affects interpretation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be combined with acetyltransferases in engineering strategies, but uncertain substrate preference across taxa should be considered
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes that many historical assays used crude homogenates without MAO inhibitors, complicating interpretation of serotonin methylation.
